1: The superscription ʾăšer hāyâ dĕbar YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû ʿal-dibrê habbaṣṣārôt — "the word that came to Jeremiah concerning the drought(s)"; baṣṣārôt (plural of baṣṣōret) denotes droughts or famines — the plural may suggest multiple drought events. The MKT renders "the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD concerning the drought" treating the plural as a collective.
2: ʾābĕlâ Yĕhûdâ — "Judah mourns"; ʾābal is the verb for formal mourning, used of communities in distress. "City gates droop in gloom" (šaʿărêhā ʾumĕlālû): ʾāmal means to languish, droop. The personification of the gates as mourners makes the whole city a unified mourning body. The MKT follows the MT's striking personification rather than flattening it.
3: The social structure of the drought revealed: officials (ʾaddîrêhem, the great ones) send servants (ṣĕʿîrêhem, the lesser ones) to cisterns that are empty. The shame gesture "cover their heads" (ḥāpû rōʾšām) indicates grief or humiliation. All social levels are equally helpless before the covenant curse of drought.
4: kî lōʾ hāyâ gešem — "because there has been no rain"; the explicit causal statement of the drought. The farmers "cover their heads" in the same gesture as the servants in Jeremiah 14:3 — the drought levels social distinctions in shared despair. ʿAdāmâ ḥattâ (the ground is cracked/dismayed) — the same verb ḥātat used for human dismay applies to the land.
5: The natural world used as evidence: even the nursing doe (ʾayyālâ baśśādeh, doe in the field) abandons her newborn (nāṭĕšâ = abandoned, forsaken) because there is no grass. Animals do not abandon their young except under extreme conditions — the drought is so severe it overrides maternal instinct. The MKT preserves the emotional force of this image.
6: pĕrāʾîm ʿāmdû ʿal-šĕpāyim — "the wild donkeys stand on the barren ridges"; wild donkeys were known for their sharp eyes and ability to find water; now they gasp for air like jackals (šāʾăpû rûaḥ kattannîm). "Their eyes grow dim" (kālû ʿênêhem) — the failure of keen eyesight signals total ecological collapse.
7: ʿăśēh lĕmaʿan šĕmekā — "act for the sake of your name"; the appeal to YHWH's own reputation grounds Israel's lament. Mĕšûbôtênû rabbû lĕkā ḥāṭāʾnû — "our unfaithfulness is many times over; we have sinned against you." The confession is corporate and unqualified. The MKT preserves the name-appeal as the primary petitionary basis distinct from Israel's merit.
8: miqweh Yiśrāʾēl — "O hope of Israel"; miqweh means both hope and a pool/reservoir of water — a deliberate wordplay in the drought context. YHWH is Israel's true water-source while the cisterns are empty. "Like a foreigner in the land, like a traveler who stops only for the night" — the accusation that YHWH has become a transient, not a permanent dweller. The MKT preserves the "hope" rendering but the water-pun enriches it for readers aware of the drought context.
9: kĕgibbôr lōʾ-yûkal lĕhôšîaʿ — "like a warrior who is powerless to save"; the bold challenge questioning YHWH's power. The prayer then recovers: "You are still in the midst of us, LORD, and we bear your name." The tension between accusation and reaffirmation is characteristic of biblical lament. The MKT preserves "in the midst of us" (bĕqirbēnû) as the ground for the continued plea.
10: YHWH's response refuses the lament: "They have loved to wander" (ʾāhăbû lānûaʿ) — their wandering after idols is a chosen lifestyle, not an involuntary drift. Lōʾ-ḥāśĕkû ragĕlêhem — "they have not restrained their feet." The MKT's "loved to wander" preserves the volitional dimension — this is desire, not weakness.
11: YHWH's second prohibition of intercession (cf. Jeremiah 7:16): ʾal-titpallēl bĕʿad hāʿām hazzeh lĕṭôbâ — "Do not pray for the welfare of this people." The phrase lĕṭôbâ (for good/welfare) specifies what is being forbidden — not all prayer, but petitions for blessing. The judgment is now irreversible; intercession would only oppose YHWH's just decree.
12: The three channels of approach to YHWH — fasting (ṣûm), burnt offerings (ʿōlâ), and grain offerings (minḥâ) — will all be ignored. The covenant's normal means of approach are suspended. The MKT preserves all three ritual forms to show the comprehensiveness of the suspension: no ritual avenue remains open.
13: Jeremiah's objection: the false prophets are promising peace and security — the people are being misled, not simply disobeying. Ḥereb lōʾ-tirʾû ... šālôm ʾĕmet ʾettēn lākem — "You will not face war... I will give you lasting peace." The word ʾĕmet (truth, reliability) placed in the false prophets' mouths is particularly ironic — they claim their peace is the real/true peace.
14: šeqer hanněbîʾîm nibbāʾîm — "The prophets are prophesying falsehoods (šeqer) in my name"; three parallel divine disavowals: "I did not send them (lōʾ šĕlaḥtîm), I did not command them (wĕlōʾ ṣiwwîtîm), and I did not speak to them (wĕlōʾ dibbartî ʾălêhem)." The triple negation is emphatic. The MKT preserves all three verbs of sending/commanding/speaking.
15: The judgment on the false prophets mirrors what they falsely promised: they promised no sword or famine; they will die by sword and famine. The poetic justice of this reversal is intentional — the prophets who denied the covenant curses will personally experience them. The MKT preserves the verbal echo: same terms (ḥereb and rāʿāb) in judgment that were denied in their oracles.
16: The people who trusted the false prophets are thrown into the streets — the reverse of the temple-court security they were promised. Wĕhišpaktî ʿălêhem ʾet-rāʿātām — "I will pour out their wickedness upon them"; the judgment is a pouring-out that mirrors the rain withheld in the drought. The MKT's "pour out" preserves this ironic contrast.
17: tērdanâ ʿênî dimʿâ laylâ wĕyômām — "Let my eyes overflow with tears night and day without stopping"; YHWH (or the prophet) commanded to weep. The phrase ʾal-tiddōm (without stopping, without ceasing) intensifies the mourning to uninterrupted grief. "The virgin daughter of my people" (bat-ʿammî) — a personification of the nation as a young woman struck down.
18: ûbāʾû ʾel-ʾereṣ — "Both prophet and priest have gone to a land they do not know"; the religious leaders are already going into exile before the final judgment. The verse is difficult: the MKT renders "both the prophet and the priest ply their trade in a land they do not know" — possibly meaning they practice their false trade even in exile. The uncertainty of this verse is acknowledged in the MKT rendering.
19: hāmāʾōs māʾasta ʾet-Yĕhûdâ — "Have you completely rejected Judah?" — the infinitive absolute (māʾōs māʾasta, "in rejecting have you rejected") conveys absolute rejection. "Does your very being recoil from Zion?" (bĕṣiyyôn gāʿălâ napšekā) — gāʿal means to loathe/abhor; the question dares to say YHWH's nepeš (inner self/being) is revolted by Zion.
20: yādaʿnû YHWH rišʿēnû — "We acknowledge, O LORD, our own wickedness"; the corporate confession moves from questioning (Jeremiah 14:19) to acknowledgment. The dual confession: our own wickedness (rišʿēnû) and our ancestors' sins (ʿăwōn ʾăbôtênû). The MKT preserves both the first-person and ancestral dimensions of the confession.
21: lĕmaʿan šĕmekā — "for the sake of your name"; the dual appeal of Jeremiah 14:21: name (šēm) and covenant (bĕrît). "Do not dishonor the throne of your glory" (kissēʾ kĕbôdekā) — the temple as YHWH's earthly throne is implicated in Israel's fate; its destruction would dishonor YHWH. The MKT preserves this implicit argument: YHWH's honor is at stake in Israel's survival.
22: hăyēš bahabrê haggôyim mağšîmîm — "Are there any among the idols of the nations that can make it rain?" The drought context makes the idol-powerlessness argument concrete: can idols control rain? The rhetorical answer is no. Hălōʾ ʾattâ-hûʾ YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — "You are the one, O LORD our God." The monotheistic confession arises from the crisis of the drought.
